preFix.title: Card-weaving double-face technique
preFix.subtitle: Producing reversible patterns with two warp colours on four-hole cards
preFix.excerpt: Double-face card weaving uses two contrasting warp colours threaded one per pair of holes on each card. Turning the cards forward brings one colour to the surface; turning backward brings the other. Patterning comes entirely from which cards turn which direction on each row.
preFix.sourceNotes:
- Collingwood, Peter. The Techniques of Tablet Weaving. Faber and Faber, 1982. In copyright; cited as the standard reference for double-face card weaving. Content written around this source, not derived from it.
- General tablet-weaving mechanics are in the public domain via multiple pre-1928 sources.

content.text: is one of the most rewarding advanced card-weaving structures. Because the two
colours invert between face and back, every pattern appears in reverse colour on the opposite side. The structure produces a dense, firm band with two fully finished faces.

items: Four-hole weaving cards (tablets), one per warp unit
Two contrasting warp yarn colours (wool or cotton, approximately 2-ply)
Weft thread (the same yarn as the warp or finer)
A card-turning sequence chart (drafted before you begin)
Warp board or two posts for measuring the warp (optional but helpful)

content.text: Each card has four holes labelled A, B, C, D. In
threading, colour 1 goes through holes A and D; colour 2 goes through holes B and C. This pairing means a quarter-turn of the card moves either two colour-1
ends or two colour-2 ends to the top of the
, controlling which colour shows on the face.
Step 1: Cut warp threads to the planned band length plus 40 cm for finishing. For each card, cut two lengths of colour 1 and two of colour 2.

Step 2: Thread hole A with colour 1, hole B with colour 2, hole C with colour 2, hole D with colour 1. Pull all four threads through from the same face of the card (S-threading). Some patterns alternate S and Z threading per card; this is noted on the draft.
Step 3: Thread all cards before securing. Line the cards up flat and align all threads so the card faces point the same direction.
Step 4: Tie one end of the warp bundle to a fixed post or your belt (backstrap style). Keep the cards at an even tension before beginning.

draft is a grid. Each column represents one card; each row is one pick. A filled square means turn that card forward (away from you) one quarter-turn. An empty square means turn backward. The pattern on the face of the band is the inverse of the draft grid itself, with colour 1 showing on forward turns and colour 2 on backward.
After each quarter-turn on all cards, open the
through. Beat firmly with a fingernail or a small bone pick so each row of
Turning all cards consistently in one direction causes
behind the cards. There are two practical approaches:
Plan the pattern so forward and backward turns are roughly balanced over the length of the band. When the draft has equal turns in both directions the twist cancels itself.

Where the pattern requires many consecutive forward turns, stop every 6-8 rows and slide the cards back along the warp to let the accumulated twist distribute. Alternatively, use a short section of background pattern in the opposite direction to untwist.

on paper before you begin. Mark each row with a horizontal line; each column is one card. Use F for forward and B for backward. Tick off each row as you complete it. For a first project, choose a simple motif of no more than 8 cards and a 12-row repeat so the draft is easy to hold in memory after a few repeats.

items.problem: Colour 2 appears on the face when colour 1 should be there
items.solution: The card is threaded with the S/Z direction reversed, or the card is upside down relative to the others. Remove the card, rethread with holes A and D holding colour 1, and confirm all cards face the same direction.

items.problem: The band is curling badly to one side
items.solution: Twist build-up is pulling the warp. Slide the cards back to release tension in the warp, or reverse the turning direction for several rows using background-colour turns to counteract the accumulated twist.

items.problem: The weft is showing on the face
items.solution: You are not beating firmly enough, or the warp sett is too loose for the yarn weight. Use a slightly heavier warp yarn, or beat each row twice, the second beat immediately after passing the weft.
